canonical_title: UK FCA review urges considering regulation of general-purpose AI models in financial services
aliases: FCA regulate ChatGPT Claude Gemini financial advice
Sheldon Mills AI financial services review
UK financial regulator general-purpose AI tools
ChatGPT Claude Gemini consumer financial decisions
treatment: Treat as UK financial-services AI regulation watch. Do not repeat unless FCA, UK government, Bank of England, or another primary regulator announces concrete perimeter changes, model-testing requirements, enforcement, consultation, or binding rules for general-purpose AI tools used in financial advice or customer-facing financial services.
known_facts: Reuters reported on 2026-07-06 that FCA executive director Sheldon Mills said Britain should review whether large language models such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini should be regulated as general-purpose AI tools as they influence consumer financial decisions.

The Reuters report said the review found more than a quarter of UK consumers trust ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini for financial advice despite limited awareness that regulated financial-services protections do not extend to those services.
The report highlighted concentration risk from financial firms relying on a small number of model, cloud, or technology providers for critical operational capabilities.
No immediate binding rule, enforcement action, or company-specific product change was reported at first coverage.
reinclude_only_if: The FCA or UK government opens a formal consultation or announces concrete regulatory perimeter changes for general-purpose AI models.
Binding requirements are proposed or adopted for model testing, financial advice boundaries, critical third-party oversight, or customer-facing AI in financial services.
OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, banks, or cloud providers make a material policy, product, or access-control change in response.
A credible source reports enforcement, supervisory action, or a significant correction to the FCA review interpretation.
notes: Midday delta item because it turns frontier/general-purpose AI tools from consumer productivity products into a financial regulatory perimeter question. Coverage is policy-directional, not a binding rule yet.
